abstract: While iron is an ideal candidate to replace precious metals in photoactive compounds, developing emissive and reactive iron complexes remains a significant challenge due to the incredibly rapid deactivation of their charge-transfer states. To date, emissive iron compounds are exceptionally rare, and dual emission has never been observed. In this work, we report the synthesis and characterization of a cyclometalated Fe(III) complex, [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆], which exhibits an unprecedented "Janus-type" dual emission originating from both ligand-to-metal (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states. This unique photophysical profile is enabled by a specialized ligand framework that integrates four N-heterocyclic carbenes with two cyclometalating aryl units. The low-lying π* levels provided by the cyclometalating units create energetically accessible MLCT states that, crucially, do not deactivate into lower-energy LMCT pathways. With an exceptionally long lifetime of 4.6 ns, the MLCT-dominated state possesses potent reducing and oxidizing power capable of driving bimolecular electron transfer. These findings establish a new design strategy for iron-based luminophores and suggest a viable path for their application in sustainable photoredox catalysis.
